blocks.text: Git Happens
by Dan Fakkeldy
Version Control for People Who Ship Software
Chapter 0 - Why Listen to a Book About Git?

Picture a Tuesday night. You've spent three days on something you're quietly proud of. It works. You've watched it work with your own eyes. And then, because you're a tidy sort of person, you make one last little change — rename a thing, move a function, tighten a margin. Something small. Something that could not possibly matter. You hit save, you run it again, and the whole thing falls over. Not a polite error, either. A cascade. Red where there used to be green, and a sinking feeling in your stomach that you've felt before and hoped never to feel again.

Now here's the cruel part. You don't know what you broke. You changed one small thing — but to undo it, you'd have to remember every other thing you touched over three days, and put it all back exactly the way it was. And you can't. Nobody can. That's not a character flaw. Human working memory holds about a handful of things at once — four, maybe seven on a good day, the number of items you can keep in your head while you walk to the kitchen and try to remember why you went in there. A real software project holds hundreds of files, and each of those files holds hundreds of small decisions, made over weeks, most of them forgotten the moment after you made them. The mismatch isn't close. It's not even in the same postcode.

And notice what your instinct does, in that moment of panic, when you don't have the right tool. You start making copies. You duplicate the whole folder before you dare touch anything else, and you name the copy something hopeful, like "working backup." You leave files half-commented-out so you can put them back the way they were, just in case. You paste old chunks of code into a notes app, like squirreling away acorns. None of it really works — you can never quite reconstruct the exact good state, and the copies pile up until you can't tell which one was the one that ran. That flailing instinct is the right impulse aimed at the wrong tool. You are trying, by brute force and sticky tape, to build yourself a memory of your own work. Git is that memory, built properly, by people who hit this exact wall a thousand times before you did.

So let's get the framing right from the very first minute, because the wrong framing will haunt you. The reason you lost an evening to a one-line change is not that you were careless. It's that you're a human being, and you were asked to do a thing human beings cannot do — hold an entire codebase in your head and reason about all of it at once. Version control, which is the family of tools this whole book is about, is not a punishment for sloppiness. It's a prosthetic for a limitation every single one of us shares. It's a tool for being human. It remembers so you don't have to. It lets you say, with total confidence, "show me exactly what I changed, and let me undo just this," and then it does precisely that. The panic you felt on Tuesday night is the exact feeling this tool was invented to abolish. Sit with that for a second, because everything that follows is downstream of it.

You may already know Git a little. Most people do, in a sideways sort of way. You've copied a few magic phrases off a website at two in the morning. You typed something, it asked you for a message, you typed "stuff" because you were tired, and it worked, and you never thought about it again. Maybe someone stood over your shoulder once and said "now push," so you pushed, with no idea where it went or why. That's not nothing. That's how almost everyone meets Git — as a set of incantations you recite to make the angry computer happy. You know it adjacent. You know the shape of it from the outside.

There's no shame in any of that, by the way. It's how most of us start, and it gets real work done. But it's a brittle way to live, because the moment something goes sideways — and something always eventually goes sideways — incantations don't help you. You can't reason your way out of trouble with a phrase you don't understand. You just type it again, harder, and hope. The people who seem calm around Git aren't calm because they've memorized more phrases than you. They're calm because they understand the small handful of ideas the phrases are made of, so when the situation is new they can reason from the ideas instead of fishing for the right spell.

This book is not here to hand you more incantations. There are already excellent places to look up exactly which words to type in which order, and we'll point at them, by name, when the moment comes. What's usually missing — the thing that turns Git from a source of dread into a source of calm — is the why underneath the words. Why you'd want a separate copy of your work to experiment in. Why a good description of a change is worth more than the change itself, sometimes. Why serious teams refuse to let anything reach their users until it has proven itself first. That's the why this book is for. No syntax diagrams that look like railroad tracks drawn by someone who hates you. No reference tables to memorize. The official documentation is the dictionary — every word, defined, alphabetical, complete, and frankly a little dry. This book is the part where you learn to write the novel. You can own a dictionary your whole life and still never write a sentence worth reading. We're after the sentences.

And here's the choice that makes this book different from most. We are not going to teach Git through made-up toy examples — through a pretend project called "my-app" that does nothing and goes nowhere. We're going to teach the whole thing through one real piece of software, that one real person actually built, ships to actual humans, and maintains in public to this day. Its name is Echo.

Let me introduce you, because you're going to spend the whole book together. Echo is an audiobook study player — an app for listening to books closely, the way a student studies, not just for passing the time on a commute. It runs on the iPhone, on the Apple Watch, on the Mac, and it even has a little Widget that sits on your home screen, one of those small panels that shows you something useful without you opening anything. It's open source, which means anyone can read every line of how it's made — the whole thing lives in public on GitHub, the website where the world's code is kept and shared, under the name Echo — and we'll get into exactly what that openness costs and grants. And the person who built it isn't a famous engineer at a famous company. He's a working mail carrier named Dan, who built Echo with AI helping at his elbow, in the margins of an ordinary life — early mornings, odd evenings, the cracks between a real job and everything else. I tell you that not as a feel-good detail but because it matters to how you should hear this whole book. If the disciplines we're about to learn were only worth it for big teams with big budgets, a solo mail carrier wouldn't bother with them. He does bother. That tells you something.

And Echo is not a sketch. It's a substantial, lived-in thing. By the time we're talking, it has somewhere around a thousand and seventy saved checkpoints on its main line of development — we'll learn the real word for those soon. It has eighty-one separate batches of finished work that were reviewed and folded in. Its little internal database has been carefully upgraded twenty-three times as the app grew, each upgrade numbered in order, version one all the way through version twenty-three, all of them kept tidily in a single folder, named for what it holds — the migrations folder, where a migration is just the careful, recorded step of moving the database from one shape to the next without losing anyone's data. The app isn't even at its grand version one yet — it's at version zero point six, still pre-release, still becoming itself.

And here's a thing worth knowing about Echo from the start, because it tells you how real it is. It isn't one app. It's what's called a monorepo — one single project folder that holds several programs that all live and grow together. Inside it there's the iPhone app, and a separate Mac app, and the little app that runs on the Apple Watch, and that home-screen Widget, and underneath all of them a shared core — a common module they all build from, so the parts they have in common are written once and reused everywhere. There's even a folder of helper scripts, written in a language called Python, for turning audio into text, and a documentation folder that quietly becomes a website. One folder. Many moving parts. All of it tracked by Git, all of it at once. If that sounds like a lot to keep straight — it is, and that's exactly why the discipline matters. Echo would not survive as a one-person project without it.

Everything we teach, we'll point at a real moment in this real project's life. Real saved work. Real parallel lines of development. A real assembly line that checks the app automatically. There was even a day when Dan changed the app's legal license — the rules for how others may use his code — from one kind of open license to another, in one clean, deliberate move. We'll come back to that small, sharp moment later, because it's a perfect little story about doing one thing on purpose and recording it forever. For now, just file Echo away as a real friend with a real history. We'll learn Git by watching how Echo actually got made.
Four ideas are going to keep coming back, chapter after chapter, like recurring characters. I want to name them plainly now, once each, so you'll recognize them when they walk back on stage.

The first: every saved checkpoint tells a story, and you are its author. The history of a project isn't a log that merely happens to you while you work. It's something you write, on purpose, for someone to read later. Usually that someone is you, six months from now, baffled and grateful.

The second: working alone does not mean working sloppily. The same care that professionals use on big teams scales all the way down to a team of one. Your future self is a collaborator too — maybe the most important one you'll ever have — and you owe that person clear, honest work.

The third: Git is a safety net, and a safety net is what lets you be braver, not more timid. Once you trust that you can always get back to solid ground, you'll try the bigger, riskier, more interesting thing. The net isn't there to make you cautious. It's there to make you bold.

And the fourth, which is newer to this book and which we'll lean on most: ship like a senior team, even when the team is just you. Real software doesn't leap from a developer's laptop straight into a stranger's hands. It climbs a ladder, one rung at a time, and nothing reaches actual users until it has earned the next rung. In Echo, that ladder has three rungs with real names — nightly, then weekly, then main. New work lands first on nightly, the rough-and-ready rung, the place where every fresh piece of work gets folded in and where things are allowed to be a little messy for a day. A test build goes out from nightly every single day, so Dan can actually live with the new code on his own phone before anyone else does. Once that work has settled and proven it isn't going to fall over, it gets promoted up to weekly — the calmer beta rung, with more time to soak, that goes out to testers on Mondays. And only the weekly that has genuinely held together for that soak gets promoted, at last, to main, the stable rung that real people download from the App Store. Up, and only up — work never slides back down the ladder. We'll spend real time on this, the promotion ladder, because learning to ship this way is what separates "it works on my machine" from "it works for everyone." The rungs are there so that a careful person, working alone, can still let each change prove itself before it's allowed to matter.

A word on how to listen, because this is a long one and you should use it the way it's built to be used. Don't try to memorize the exact words to type. Honestly — don't. You'll forget them, and that's fine, because they're a search away the instant you need them. Aim instead at the why. The why is sticky. Once you understand why a separate line of development is safe, you'll never again need someone to remind you it exists. The chapters do build on each other, so going in order the first time through is the gentle path. But every chapter also stands on its own two feet, which means later — months from now, when life hands you the exact situation a chapter is about — you can come straight back to that one chapter and find it waiting, complete, no re-reading the whole book required. And you do not have to do this in one sitting. Nobody learns a craft in one sitting. Listen to a chapter on a walk, let it settle, come back tomorrow.

So what will you actually be able to do, when the last chapter ends? Concretely, this. You'll be able to take any project that lives online and make your own working copy of it on your machine — and you'll know the word for that and why it's safe. You'll branch without that little flinch of fear, spinning off a private place to try something wild with the calm certainty that the original is untouched. You'll write descriptions of your changes that actually tell a story, so that the history of your work reads like a record someone could follow, instead of a wall of "fixed stuff" and "more changes." You'll open up a clean, reviewable bundle of work — a pull request, we'll learn — and, just as importantly, aim it at the right rung of the ladder, which is almost never the top one. You'll know, for instance, that a brand-new piece of work almost never aims at the top of the ladder — that in Echo you'd point it at nightly, the bottom rung, and let it climb, and you'll understand in your bones why aiming straight at main would be reckless even when you're sure your code is fine. You'll review other people's work, and your own, with both insight and kindness, because those two things are not opposites. You'll recover from your own mistakes without panic, calmly, the way someone does when they know the net is really there. And you'll ship on a real promotion ladder, the way a professional does, letting your work prove itself one rung at a time instead of betting everything on a single nervous leap.

Let me give you one small taste of that calm right now, because it's the moment people fall in love with this tool. Imagine you go down a path with your code, and an hour later you realize the whole path was a mistake — a dead end, a bad idea, the kind of thing that used to mean undoing a dozen scattered edits by hand and praying you got them all. Think back to that Tuesday night at the start of this chapter, the one-line change and the cascade of red. In the old world, that night cost you an evening of detective work. In the new world, it costs you one command. With Git, you tell it, in one short instruction, to throw that detour away and put you back exactly where you stood before you started — back to the last good checkpoint you saved. And it does. In under a second. The dead end vanishes as if it never happened, and the real, working version is sitting there, pristine, waiting for you. The first time you feel that — one command, one second, a bad hour erased with no damage done — something shifts in how you work. You stop tiptoeing around your own code like it's made of glass. You start poking at it, trying the wild idea, because you know the way back is free. That's the entire promise of this book, compressed into a single second.

Here's where all of this lands. Version control isn't a fact you memorize once and file away. It's a practice — something you do, a little better each week, the way you'd practice an instrument or a sport. You won't be perfect at it in a week, and you don't need to be. You just need to be a little more deliberate today than you were yesterday, and Echo's whole thousand-commit history is proof that small, steady deliberateness, repeated, adds up to something real. Everything in the rest of this book is in service of one humble, enormous goal: making the two words git commit — the small act of telling Git "save this, and here's what it means" — actually count for something. To turn that quiet little keystroke from a thing you do nervously, with a tired one-word message, into a thing you do on purpose, with a story attached that the next person will be glad you wrote. We'll get there step by step, in Echo's good company.

But before any of that, we have to go back to the bad old days — to the way people saved their work before Git existed at all. The folder full of files named "final," and "final version two," and "final actually use this one," and "final FOR REAL." You've made that folder. We all have. That's where we're headed next, into the save-as graveyard, so you can feel in your bones exactly what Git came to rescue us from.
Chapter 1 - Before Git: The Save-As Graveyard

Open your Documents folder. Go on, picture it. Somewhere in there, I promise, lives a small cemetery. You have a file called "draft." Next to it, "draft version two." Then "final." Then, because the world is cruel, "final really final." And then, the headstone of headstones, a file named "final, do not open, this one is old," which is, of course, the very first thing you double-click the next time you go looking. You open it anyway. You always open it anyway.

This is the Save-As graveyard, and you did not invent it. Nobody taught it to you. It grew, the way weeds grow, in the cracks of a perfectly ordinary afternoon. You finished something. You were a little bit proud of it. And then you needed to change it, but you weren't quite sure the change was a good idea, so you did the sensible, frightened thing: you made a copy first. "Save As." A new name. A safety rope thrown to your future self. And then you did it again the next day, and the day after, and within a week you had a row of nearly identical files, each one a slightly different version of the same idea, none of them clearly labeled, all of them lying to you a little.

And here's the part that should make you feel better, not worse: everybody does this. This is not a programmer problem. I have watched a wedding photographer do it with a folder of edits. I have watched a novelist do it with chapters, and a small-business owner do it with a spreadsheet of prices, and a teacher do it with a lesson plan that mutated, over three years, into eleven files with the word "updated" in them at increasingly desperate frequencies. The instinct is good. The instinct is, honestly, correct. You should keep your old work before you change it. The instinct is right. The filing system is what's broken.

Because the graveyard works. That's the trap. For a while, it genuinely works. You can usually find yesterday's version, and last week's is probably still around, and if you squint at the names you can reconstruct roughly what happened. It works right up until the exact moment you need it most.

Here is that moment. It's late. Let's say two in the morning, because that's when these things happen. You are deep in some change, and it is going badly, and you decide what you really want is the version from about a month ago, the one from before all of this, when things were calm and worked. So you go to the graveyard. And you find "final." And you find "final really final." And you find "draft version two." And you cannot, for the life of you, remember which one was the good one from last month. You open three of them. They all look almost the same. One has the paragraph you wanted but also a mistake you'd fixed. One has the fix but not the paragraph. And the version you actually want, the real one, the calm one from a month ago? You overwrote it. You saved on top of it at some point, with the best of intentions, and it is simply gone. Not in the trash. Not recoverable. Gone, because past-you needed the disk space or the tidy folder more than future-you needed the history.

This is the thing the graveyard can never fix, no matter how clever your names get. Humans are genuinely bad at naming things, and we are even worse at remembering, weeks later, what a past version of ourselves meant by a name. "Final" meant something specific and urgent on the Tuesday you typed it. By the following month it means nothing. It's a word you left on a tombstone, and the person who could have explained it has moved on. You are not careless. You are normal. The tool is asking you to be a kind of librarian that no human actually is.

And notice what the dates can't save you, either. You might think, fine, I'll ignore the names and just sort by the date the file was last touched. But the dates lie too, in their own way. The newest file is whichever one you opened most recently, not the one that holds the best version of your work. The moment you double-click "final, do not open, this one is old" just to peek inside, its timestamp jumps to today, and now the oldest, most abandoned draft looks freshest of all. Every time you glance at the graveyard, you disturb it a little, like footprints in snow you were trying to read. The information you need is smeared by the very act of looking for it. There is no column you can sort by that tells you the one thing you actually want to know, which is: which of these was the good one, and what was I thinking when I made it?
Now, you might be thinking: but I have backups. My whole machine copies itself to a drive in the corner, or up to some service in the sky, every night while I sleep. Doesn't that solve it?

It does not, and the difference is worth getting exactly right, because people conflate these two things constantly and then wonder why their backup didn't save them. A backup is a photograph of your whole machine at one moment in time. It captures everything, all at once, as it was at, say, three in the morning. That's useful. If your laptop is stolen or your hard drive dies, a backup is what brings your life back. But a backup answers only one question: what did everything look like at this single instant? It cannot tell you what changed between Tuesday and Wednesday. It cannot tell you why you changed it. It cannot show you the one paragraph that moved, or let you take just that paragraph back while keeping everything else. A backup is a snapshot of the whole house. It is not a record of who moved the furniture, or when, or what they were trying to do.

Version control is the other thing. Version control is a time-lapse, not a photograph. It records the changes themselves, in order, as a continuous story: what changed, when it changed, who changed it, and, if you're doing it right, why. Picture the difference between a single still photo of your living room and a time-lapse film of the whole renovation, every piece of furniture sliding in and out, the walls changing color, captioned at each step with a note from the person doing the work. The backup is the photo. Version control is the film. You want both, for different reasons, but only one of them lets you stand inside your own history and walk around.

The tool that gives you that film, for software and for plenty of things that aren't software, is called Git. It's a small, fast program that runs on your own computer, and its entire job is to remember the history of a project so you don't have to. That's it. That's the pitch. It is the thing that finally lets you delete the graveyard and stop being afraid.

Let me give you the simplest possible picture of how Git thinks, because once it clicks, a lot of mystery falls away. You might assume Git works by writing down a list of changes, like an editor's tracked edits, a long scroll of "removed this word, added that line." Reasonable guess. Wrong, though, and the truth is actually friendlier. Git does not store change-lists. Git stores snapshots.

Every time you tell Git to save your progress, with a command called git commit, it takes a complete picture of your entire project at that moment. The whole thing. Every file, exactly as it stands. Each one of those saved pictures is called a commit, and you make one whenever you've finished a thought worth keeping. Now, you might worry that storing the entire project over and over would balloon to a monstrous size, and Git worried about that too, so it's clever in one specific way: if a file hasn't changed since the last snapshot, Git doesn't copy it again. It just points back at the version it already has, the way you might say "same as before" instead of writing the whole thing out twice. So each commit is a full, complete picture of the project, but it costs almost nothing to keep, because the parts that didn't move aren't stored twice. You get the honesty of a full snapshot at the price of just the differences.

And every commit carries a little note, written by you, in plain language. A message. Something like "added the search box," or "fixed the date that showed up wrong on the watch." That message is the caption on that frame of the time-lapse. It's the thing your future self, at two in the morning a month from now, will read with enormous gratitude, because it tells them exactly what this version was for. This is the first place that idea I'll keep coming back to shows up: a commit is something you write on purpose, as the author of your own history, not a log that merely happens to you. You decide where the chapters break. You decide what each one says.

And here's a quiet thing worth noticing about that, even on your very first day, even working entirely alone. The caption you write isn't really for the person typing it. You already know what you just did; you don't need a note to remember a change you made thirty seconds ago. The caption is a letter to a stranger, and the stranger is you, a month from now, who has forgotten everything. The discipline of writing a clear message for that future stranger is exactly the discipline a professional team uses so its members can read each other's work, and it scales all the way down to a team of one. Working alone is not an excuse to be sloppy. It's just the case where the only collaborator you're being kind to, or unkind to, happens to be yourself.

Once your project is a string of these captioned snapshots, the things you can do feel a little like time travel. You can revisit any snapshot, pull up the project exactly as it was three weeks and forty saves ago, look around, and come back. You can put two snapshots side by side and ask Git to show you precisely what differs between them, down to the single line. You can start a new line of work from any point in the past, try something wild, and if it's a disaster, simply walk away from it with the rest of your history untouched. And you can take two separate lines of work and weave them back together. You'll meet all of those properly in the chapters ahead. For now, just sit with the shape of it: no more graveyard, no more guessing. Every version you ever cared about is saved, labeled, and reachable.

Let me make this concrete, because abstractions about snapshots only get you so far, and there's a real app behind this whole book that lives and breathes by exactly this. The app is called Echo. It's a real, open-source audiobook study player, the kind of thing you'd use to listen to a book and follow along, running on the iPhone, the Apple Watch, and the Mac, with a little widget on the home screen. It was built by one person, a working mail carrier, with some help from AI, in the spare margins of an ordinary life. As I'm telling you this, Echo's history holds something like a thousand and seventy saved snapshots on its main line of work. That's a thousand-plus captioned frames of a time-lapse, and not one of them is a file named "final really final."

Here's the part of Echo that shows you why this matters. Like most apps, Echo keeps its information in a small database on your device, and the shape of that database, what gets stored and how, has changed and grown as the app learned to do more. Each time that shape changes, you can't just flip a switch; you have to carefully move everyone's existing data over to the new shape without losing anything. That careful move is called a migration, and Echo has done it more than twenty times. Version one, version two, on up through version twenty-three, each one a deliberate, recorded step. They all live together in a folder named, plainly, the Migrations folder, tucked inside the shared part of the project, and each one is a real moment in the app's growth. One migration added the ability to store a book's table of contents as a proper nested outline, chapters and the sections within them. Another added a place to keep narration voices right there on the device. Another taught the database to remember word-by-word read-along timing, so the app can highlight each word exactly as it's spoken. Another built an overnight queue on the Mac, so a batch of work could grind away while its owner slept.

Now imagine doing that without Git. Twenty-three changes to the most delicate part of your app, the part that holds all the user's data, and your only record of which change shipped in which version of the app is... a spreadsheet you keep by hand. A spreadsheet you have to remember to update, every time, forever, while tired. You'd be one forgotten row away from shipping a migration twice, or skipping one, or shipping the wrong one to the wrong place and quietly corrupting somebody's library. With Git, there's no spreadsheet to forget, because the history is the record. Every one of those migrations is a commit. Each one already says, in its own caption and its own timestamp, exactly when it happened, who made it, and what feature it was for. The record isn't a chore you maintain alongside the work. The record is the work, remembered honestly.

There's a second story from Echo that shows off a different muscle, and it's one of those things that sounds tedious until you realize Git makes it almost relaxing. Early on, Echo carried one kind of open-source license, a permissive one often called the MIT license. Later, its owner decided to switch to a different, stronger one, the GPL, the kind that keeps the code open as it travels. Now, a license isn't just one file. By convention, a short license notice sits at the very top of every single source file in the project, and Echo has hundreds of them. So changing the license meant editing a little block of text at the top of hundreds of files at once. That is exactly the kind of sweeping, mechanical, easy-to-botch change that keeps careful people up at night. Did every file get updated? Did one get missed? Did a finger slip and break something?

With Git, you don't lie awake wondering. The whole sweep becomes a single commit, one captioned frame in the time-lapse that says, in effect, "changed the license across the project." And because Git can show you exactly what differs, you can look at that one snapshot and see every file that was touched, all of them, side by side, and confirm with your own eyes that each one got the new notice and nothing else broke in the process. The fear of "did I get them all" turns into a thing you can simply check. That's the safety net working: not stopping you from making a big, sweeping change, but making the big change safe enough to actually do.

There's one more quality of Git I want to plant now, because it quietly shapes everything that follows, and the word for it is distributed. When you copy a Git project to your own machine, an act called cloning, you don't get just the latest version. You get the entire history. Every snapshot, every caption, all the way back to the very first one. The whole time-lapse, the complete film, sitting on your laptop. Which means a few wonderful things are suddenly true. You can work with no internet at all, saving snapshot after snapshot, building real history, with nothing connected. Echo's maker has done exactly that, committing his work in the gaps of a mail route, between one delivery stop and the next, somewhere with no signal, and Git did not care one bit. And because every clone carries the full history, every copy is a complete, self-sufficient backup of the whole story. There's no single fragile original sitting on a server somewhere that everything depends on. The history lives, in full, on every machine that has ever copied it. It's resilient the way a story told by many people is resilient. You'd have to lose all the copies at once to lose anything at all.

So that's the ground we're standing on. Version control solves a problem so basic, so woven into the texture of ordinary work, that most people never quite name it. They just feel it, as a low background hum of anxiety every time they're about to change something they care about. That little flinch before you edit. That reflex to make a copy first, just in case. That whole graveyard of "final really final" files is just fear, made visible, one tombstone at a time. And the quiet magic of what you're about to learn is that the fear has somewhere to go. When every version is saved, captioned, and reachable, you stop being afraid to change things, because nothing you've made can truly be lost anymore.

In the next chapter, you'll build the thing that holds all of this. You'll take a plain folder on your own machine and, with a command or two, turn it into a real repository, your first one, the place where the snapshots live. It's a smaller step than it sounds, and once you've taken it, the graveyard is behind you for good.
Chapter 2 - Your First Repository

Picture a folder on your computer. Maybe it's empty, maybe it already has a file or two in it. You open a terminal, you point it at that folder, and you type three small words: git init. You press return. And then... almost nothing happens. A single quiet line appears, something close to "initialized empty repository," and the cursor blinks back at you, waiting for the next thing. If you flip over to the window where you can see your files, nothing has moved. No new document you can see. No pop-up congratulating you. The folder looks exactly the way it looked a second ago.

It would be easy to assume the command didn't do much. It is, in fact, one of the most consequential things you'll do all day. Because in that ordinary-looking folder, you just installed a time machine. From this moment on, every version of every file you choose to record can be brought back, compared, and understood. The folder doesn't look different because the change isn't on the surface. It's underneath. And once you understand what just happened underneath, the rest of Git stops feeling like magic and starts feeling like a tool you can reach for on purpose.

So let's lift the hood. Just once, gently, the way you might pop the bonnet of a car to see where the engine sits before you ever try to fix it. You don't need to memorize any of this. You just need to see that it's there, so that Git stops being a black box.

When you ran git init, it created one hidden folder. Its name is a single dot followed by the word git, and out loud people just say "dot-git." The dot at the front is a quiet little convention on Mac and Linux that means "don't show this in the normal file listing." That's why you didn't see anything change. The dot-git folder is sitting right inside your project, but your computer politely keeps it out of sight so you don't trip over it every day. That one hidden folder is the entire repository. Everything Git knows about your project's past lives in there.
And inside it, three kinds of things live together. Let me name them, because naming them is how the whole system stops being mysterious.

The first is the objects database. Think of it as a private warehouse where Git keeps the actual contents of your files, every version you've ever recorded. But it doesn't store them the way you'd file papers in a cabinet, by name and date. It stores them by their contents. When you save a version of a file, Git reads the bytes inside it and runs them through a kind of mathematical blender that produces a short fingerprint, a string of characters unique to that exact content. Change a single comma and you get a completely different fingerprint. That fingerprint becomes the file's address in the warehouse. People call this content-addressable storage, which is a mouthful, but the idea underneath it is friendly. The address of a thing is derived from the thing itself.

This buys you two lovely properties for free. The first is deduplication. If two files in your project happen to be byte-for-byte identical, they produce the same fingerprint, which means the same address, which means Git only stores the content once. It's not fooled by the file having two different names in two different folders. Same contents, stored once. The second property is self-validation. Because the address is computed from the contents, Git can always re-check its own work. It can take a file out of the warehouse, recompute the fingerprint, and confirm it matches the address it was filed under. If a hard drive quietly corrupts a byte somewhere, the fingerprints stop matching and Git can tell you something is wrong. Your history can, in a real sense, check itself for damage. That's the checksum at work, an automatic integrity test baked into the design.

The second kind of thing in dot-git is references. This is a grand word for something delightfully humble. A reference is just a label that points at one particular commit, one particular saved moment in your history. Branches are references. Tags are references. And the thing to understand, the thing that makes Git feel so light once it clicks, is that a branch is not a copy of your project. A branch is a sticky note. It's a tiny label with the fingerprint of a commit written on it. When you make a new commit on that branch, Git peels the sticky note off the old commit and moves it onto the new one. That's the whole operation. Which is why making a new branch in Git is instant and costs you almost nothing. You're not duplicating files. You're writing a new sticky note. Hold on to that image. It pays off enormously in a couple of chapters when we start branching for real.

The third kind of thing is configuration. This is the settings drawer. It's where Git keeps the small facts it needs to do its job on your behalf: your name and your email address, which get stamped onto every commit you make so the history knows who did what; the name of your default branch; and later, when your project lives on the internet, the web addresses of the copies it talks to. Plain settings, quietly remembered, so you don't have to repeat yourself.

So that's the engine. An objects database that fingerprints and deduplicates and self-checks. References that are movable sticky notes pointing at commits. And configuration that remembers who you are and where things live. Three parts, all tucked inside one hidden dot-git folder.

Now here's the part that I find genuinely reassuring, and I want to make sure it lands. All of that, the entire history of your project, lives inside that one dot-git folder, sitting right beside your actual files. It's not in the cloud. It's not on some company's server you're renting. It's not something you'll lose access to if a website goes down or a subscription lapses. It's on your machine, in your folder, and you can work with it on a plane with the wi-fi off. If you were to delete the dot-git folder, you'd be left with exactly what you started with: an ordinary folder containing your files, and not one scrap of history. No versions, no record of who changed what, no time machine. Just files. The history isn't sprinkled invisibly through your documents. It lives in that one place, and you own it completely.

I point this out not to make you nervous about deleting the wrong folder, but because it tells you something true about what you've got. Your history is yours. It's local, it's complete, and it's right there. And that is the first quiet appearance of an idea that runs through this whole book: Git is a safety net that lets you be braver. From the very first time you type git init, you've got a record you can return to. You can try the bold thing, the experimental thing, the "I'm not sure this'll work" thing, because if it doesn't, the previous version is sitting safely in the warehouse, fingerprinted and waiting. The net is already under you. You just installed it in three keystrokes.

Now, git init is one of two ways a repository comes into the world, and it's the way you'd use to start something brand new. But most of the time, when you join a project that already exists, you're not starting from an empty folder. You're picking up something with a past. And for that, there's a second command: git clone.

You give git clone a web address, the location of an existing repository out on the internet, and it does three things in one breath. It makes a new folder for you. It runs the equivalent of git init inside that folder, setting up the dot-git engine we just toured. And then it reaches out across the network and downloads the complete history, every commit, every version, the whole warehouse, and tucks it into your new local copy. When it's done, you don't have a snapshot or a preview. You have the entire project and everything that ever happened to it, sitting on your own machine, ready to work with offline.

Let's make that concrete with the app we're following through this whole book: Echo. Echo is a real, open-source study player for audiobooks, the kind of thing you'd use to read along with a book while you listen. It runs on the iPhone, on the Apple Watch, on the Mac, and it even puts a little Widget on your home screen. It was built by one person, Dan, a working mail carrier, with a lot of AI assistance, in the scraps of time around the rest of his life. And it is not a small thing. When you clone Echo, here is what arrives. There's the iPhone app. There's the Mac app, which goes by the name Echo macOS. There's the watch app. There's the Widget. There's a Shared module, which holds the parts that all of those apps build on top of, the database layer, the code that reads e-book files, the core models of what a book even is. There's a Tools folder, full of Python scripts that handle transcription, turning spoken audio into text. And there's a docs folder, which quietly builds itself into a website. All of that, the whole multi-platform, multi-language thing, lands on your machine in a matter of seconds, history and all. One command, and the entire project's life is yours to explore.

When you look at those top-level folders, something nice happens. The shape of the folders teaches you the shape of the app. You can see at a glance that there's an iPhone side and a Mac side and a watch side and a Widget, and that they all lean on a shared core. You haven't read a single line of code, and already the architecture has introduced itself. That's not an accident. That's a choice.

Echo keeps everything in one repository. The iPhone app, the Mac app, the watch app, the Widget, the shared core, the Python tools, the docs, all of it under one roof. There's a word for that: a monorepo, a single repository holding what could otherwise have been split across many. And like every real engineering decision, it bought something and it cost something, and it's worth saying both plainly, once.

What it bought is unity. Because the apps share that core module, a single fix in the shared code can ripple out to every platform at the same moment. Fix a bug in how books are stored once, and the phone, the watch, the Mac, and the Widget all get the fix together, in the same commit, with no chasing it across separate projects and hoping you remembered all of them. What it cost is heft. One repository holding everything is a larger repository, with a longer history, and that means some operations have more to scan through and take a touch longer. Echo looked at that trade and decided its platforms share far more than they keep separate, so keeping them together was worth the extra weight. That's the honest shape of the decision. Not that monorepos are good or bad, but that this project's pieces belonged together more than they belonged apart.

All right. You've got a repository, whether you started it fresh or cloned it down. Before you make your very first commit, there's a small piece of housekeeping that separates the people who'll be glad later from the people who'll be sorry.

There's a file you can put in your project called dot-gitignore. Out loud it's just "dot-gitignore," and what it is, is a list of instructions telling Git what not to pay attention to. Not everything in a project folder deserves to be remembered. Some of the things sitting in there are junk that the computer generates on its own, and some of them are things that should never, ever be written down. The dot-gitignore file is where you say so.

Two big categories belong in there. The first is anything the computer makes for itself while building your app. When you compile Echo, Xcode, which is the program Apple developers build with, produces piles of intermediate files. There's a folder full of build leftovers with the unwieldy name DerivedData, and there's a build folder, and both of them are full of stuff that gets regenerated from scratch every time you build. There's no reason to record them. They're not your work; they're the byproduct of your work, like sawdust on a workshop floor. Echo's dot-gitignore tells Git to ignore DerivedData and the build folder, and the project stays clean. What Echo does keep, deliberately, is a file called Package.resolved, which pins down the exact versions of the outside libraries the app depends on, things like its database library, GRDB. That file you want to track, because it's the difference between everyone building the app against the same ingredients and everyone quietly building against slightly different ones.

The second category is the one to take seriously: secrets. A secret is anything that's meant to stay private and acts like a key, an access token, a password, an API key that lets your app talk to some paid service. Here is the trap, and it catches even careful people. Git's whole job is to remember. It is exquisitely good at not forgetting. So if you commit a secret even once, that secret is now woven into your history. Deleting it from the current version doesn't pull it out of the past; the old commit still has it, sitting right there in the warehouse, fingerprinted and preserved, for anyone who ever clones the project. The fix isn't to delete it later. The fix is to never let it in. And dot-gitignore is the gate you set up at the door, so the secret never gets recorded in the first place.

There's a tidy rule of thumb hiding in all of this, and it covers most cases you'll meet. If a file can be regenerated from your source code, you don't need to track it, because you can always make it again. And if a file is a secret, you must not track it, because the history never forgets. Regenerated, or secret: those are the two things you keep out.
Now, at last, the first commit. And to do it well, you need to know that a file in a Git project lives in one of three places, and that moving between them is the act at the center of everyday Git.

The first place is the working directory. That's just your folder as you see it, the actual files you're editing right now, with whatever changes you've made this morning. It's your desk, with papers spread across it.

The second place is the staging area, and it's the one beginners skip past too fast, so let me slow down on it. The staging area, sometimes also called the index, is a kind of waiting room, a holding tray, between your messy desk and the permanent record. When you've changed five files but only three of them belong together as one coherent idea, you put just those three in the tray. You use the command git add to do it: git add takes a change from your working directory and places it in the staging area, ready to be recorded. The other two changes stay on your desk, untouched, waiting for their own moment. The staging area is what lets you compose a commit deliberately, rather than dumping everything you've touched into one undifferentiated lump. It's the difference between writing a sentence and sneezing words onto a page.

The third place is the repository itself, the permanent record, the warehouse. And you move things from the tray into the record with one command: git commit. When you commit, Git takes everything sitting in the staging area, seals it together into a single saved moment, and asks you to attach a message describing what this change is and why you made it. That sealed, described moment is a commit. It gets its own fingerprint. It joins the history. And the sticky note for your branch peels off the old commit and lands on this new one.

I want to leave you with a way of thinking about that act, because it reframes everything that follows. A commit is not an autosave. Autosave is passive; it just captures whatever happens to be on screen, mindlessly, on a timer, whether or not it means anything. A commit is the opposite. A commit is a publication. You chose what goes in it, you sealed it on purpose, and you wrote down why. It is a small, deliberate statement to everyone who comes after you, including, and maybe especially, your own future self: here is a thing I did, here is what it was, and here is the reason. That's the first throughline of this whole book, arriving early. Every commit tells a story, and you are its author. The history isn't something that happens to you. It's something you write.

And that's worth ending on, because it tells you what you've actually built today. A repository is the most valuable thing in a software project, and it's worth understanding why. The code itself can always be rewritten; if you lost it but kept the history, you could reconstruct the path that got you here. But the history of decisions, the record of what was tried, what was chosen, what was abandoned and why, that can't be regenerated from anything. Once it's gone, it's gone. Lose the code and you've lost a draft. Lose the history and you've lost the story of how the thinking happened. So you've made the time machine, you've decided what it should and shouldn't remember, and you've learned the three-step path a change takes from your desk to the permanent record. The next thing worth getting good at is the part where you write down why, because a commit with a careless message is a story told badly, and a commit with a clear one is a gift to the person who reads it later. That person is usually you. And that's exactly where we're headed next.
Chapter 3 - Commits That Tell Stories

Picture this. It's a Tuesday night, six months from now. Something in your app is broken, and you don't know why. The player crashes, but only sometimes, and only after a phone call, and you can't make it happen on demand. So you do the sensible thing. You open the history of your project and you start reading backward through your own commits, looking for the moment something changed.
And here is what you find. A commit from last spring that says, in its entirety, "fixed stuff."

That's it. Fixed stuff. Past you, in a hurry, typed two words and moved on. And now present you is sitting there asking the screen, with real feeling, fixed what stuff? You changed something. You thought it mattered enough to save. But you left no note about what you touched or why, and so this entry in your history is worthless to you. Worse than worthless, really, because it's taking up a line and pretending to be information.

Now let me show you the other version of that same moment. Imagine instead that the commit reads, "fix crash when resuming playback after a phone call." That's the summary line, the headline. And underneath it, a few sentences: when a call comes in, the system interrupts the audio session, and when the call ends, the player wasn't reconnecting to that session, so the next tap to resume would crash. The fix re-establishes the audio session before resuming.

Read that and you're done. You don't have to spelunk through the code to reconstruct what past you was thinking, because past you told you. Thirty seconds of writing, back then, just saved present you thirty minutes of confusion. That's the trade, and it's lopsided in your favour almost every single time. A commit message is one of the highest-return things you will ever type, precisely because the cost is tiny and you pay it once, while the benefit lands later, exactly when you're stressed and stuck and grateful.

So that's the whole subject of this chapter. Not the mechanics of saving your work, which you already met. The craft of saving it well. The difference between a history that's just a pile of timestamps and a history that actually tells you the story of how your app came to be the way it is.

Let me start with the question of who you're even writing these messages for. Because it's tempting to think a commit message is for a reviewer, or for some imagined audience of other programmers peering at your project. And sometimes it is. But the person who reads your commit messages most often, by a wide margin, is you. Future you. The you of next week, next month, next year, who has completely forgotten the context that feels so obvious right now.

That's the thing about context. Right now, as you make a change, you are swimming in it. You know exactly which bug you're chasing, which weird edge case bit you, which of three possible fixes you chose and why you ruled out the other two. It's all vivid and present. And it will evaporate. Not slowly. Fast. By next month it'll be gone, and the only trace left will be whatever you bothered to write down.

So here's a way to hold it in your head. Every commit message is a letter to your future self. And future you is a real person who deserves better than a shrug. When you write the message, write the letter you would actually want to receive. Imagine opening this exact commit a year from now, knowing nothing, and ask: what would I need to be told? Write that. It's a small act of kindness across time, from the person who has all the context to the person who will have none.

And this is the first place that idea from earlier in the book shows up in a concrete way. Solo does not mean sloppy. When you're working alone it's easy to think the discipline is optional, that messages and structure are things teams need because they have to coordinate, and you don't, so why bother. But you do have a collaborator. It's future you. You are on a team with yourself across time, and you owe that teammate clear work, the same way you'd owe it to anyone else.
All right. So you're convinced the message matters. What actually makes a good one? It comes down to two things: what you put in a single commit, and how you describe it.

Let's take what goes in first, because it's the part people skip. The ideal is one commit, one logical change. The jargon for this is an atomic commit, and atomic here means indivisible, a single coherent unit that stands on its own. The picture I like is a single Lego brick. A good commit is one brick. You can snap it out of the wall later without the whole structure coming apart, because it does exactly one thing and doesn't tangle itself up with five unrelated things.

Now contrast that with how it often goes in real life. You sit down to fix a bug, and while you're in there you notice a typo, and you rename a variable that always bugged you, and you tweak some spacing, and then you fix the actual bug, and you commit the whole pile at once with a message like "fix bug and cleanup." That's four bricks fused into a blob. And the day you want to undo just the bug fix, because it turned out to be wrong, you can't, because it's welded to the typo fix and the rename and the spacing. You'd have to pull all of it or none of it.

There's a lovely simple test for this, and it's the word "and." If the summary of your commit needs the word "and" to be honest, that's a signal you've probably got more than one logical change in there, and you should split it into separate commits. Fix the bug, commit. Rename the variable, commit. Fix the typo, commit. Three bricks, each one clean, each one removable on its own. Whenever you catch yourself reaching for "and" in a commit summary, treat it as a gentle tap on the shoulder.

Now, how you describe it. A good commit message has two parts. There's the summary line, which is the headline, and then there's the body, which is the explanation. The summary line is short, around fifty characters, and the convention is to write it as a command, an instruction. Not "fixed the crash" and not "fixing the crash," but "fix the crash." As if you're telling the codebase what to do. "Add the export button." "Remove the dead code." It feels a little odd at first, but it's a small consistency that makes a long list of commits read cleanly, every line in the same grammatical key. The reason for the fifty-character target is just that these summaries show up in narrow places, in lists and side panels, and a short headline doesn't get cut off.

And then the body, which is the part that earns its keep. The summary tells you what changed. The body tells you why. And the why is almost always the part you can't reconstruct later. What the change does, you can often work out by reading the code itself. Why you made it, what was broken, what you tried that didn't work, which approach you chose and what you gave up to choose it, that lives nowhere but the body. So the body is where you write to future you about the reasoning. You don't need a body on every commit. "Fix the typo in the welcome message" needs no further explanation. But the moment there's a why worth knowing, that's where it goes.

Now, a lot of smart people have converged on a light convention for the summary line that makes all of this even more useful, and it's worth knowing by name because you'll see it everywhere. It's called Conventional Commits. And don't let the formal name scare you, because the idea is almost trivially simple. You start your summary with a type word that says what kind of change this is.

The common type words are a small vocabulary. There's feature, for when you add something new the user can see. There's fix, for fixing a bug. There's docs, short for documentation, for changes to written explanations rather than code. There's refactor, for reshaping code without changing what it does, like tidying a room without buying any new furniture. There's test, for adding or changing tests. There's perf, short for performance, for making something faster. There's style, for purely cosmetic formatting that doesn't touch behaviour. There's chore, the catch-all for routine housekeeping. There's ci, which is the letters C and I, for changes to the automation that runs on your project. And there's build, for changes to how the app gets compiled and packaged. Ten little words, and once they're in your fingers they cost nothing.

After the type word, you can add an optional scope in parentheses, which is just a short tag naming which part of the app you touched, and then a colon, and then your imperative description. So spoken out loud, a message might be "fix, player scope, reconnect the audio session after an interruption." The type tells you the kind of change, the scope tells you the neighbourhood, and the description tells you the change itself, all in one scannable line.

There's one more piece of punctuation worth knowing. If a change breaks something that used to work in a way other people relied on, a breaking change, you flag it with an exclamation mark right before the colon. It's a small loud signal that says, careful, this one isn't a quiet improvement, this one will make you change how you use the thing. Spoken aloud you'd just say "this is a breaking change," but in the written message it's that little mark, and tools and humans both learn to watch for it.

And here's the quietly clever part. Because the message follows a predictable shape, it's machine-readable as well as human-readable. A program can read through all your commits, pick out every one that starts with feature, and automatically assemble a list of new features for your release notes. That's where auto-generated changelogs come from, those tidy "what's new" lists, built straight from well-formed commit messages without anyone writing them by hand. But honestly, even if no machine ever read a single one, the convention would still earn its place, because mostly it's just for humans scanning a long list and instantly seeing the shape of the work. That's the real payoff. The automation is a bonus.
Now let me make all of this concrete, because so far it's been principles, and principles are easy to nod along to and hard to feel. Let's look at Echo's actual history.

Echo, if you're just joining, is a real open-source audiobook study player, the running example for this whole book. It runs on iPhone, Apple Watch, and Mac, with a little home-screen widget, and it was built by one person, a working mail carrier, with AI assistance, in the margins of an ordinary life. And as of now its main line of history holds a bit over a thousand commits. Call it a thousand and seventy. And every one of them follows Conventional Commits. Not most. All of them. Strictly.

And the remarkable thing is that the breakdown of those thousand-odd commits is itself a story you can read at a glance, just from the type words. More than two hundred sixty of them are features. More than two hundred are fixes. More than a hundred forty are documentation. Then a smaller pile of refactors, around fifty. Forty-some chores. A couple dozen tests. Sixteen performance changes. Fourteen of those continuous-integration changes. Nine build changes. A handful of style tweaks.

Sit with that shape for a second, because it's a portrait. Lots of features, which means the app kept growing. Almost as many fixes, which is just honest, because real software breaks and gets mended, over and over. And a genuinely large stack of documentation commits, which tells you this was a project that took explaining itself seriously, not an afterthought. You can learn the character of a project from the silhouette of its commit types, before you read a single line of code. That's history telling a story all by itself.

The scopes tell their own story too, because they read like a map of the app. There's the Mac scope, for the desktop version. There's the database scope, for where your books and progress are stored. There's the narration scope, for the engine that handles spoken text. There's the export scope, for the pipeline that gets audio out of the app. There's the player scope, the heart of actually listening. And there's the Mac batch scope, for the system on the desktop that chews through a lot of work at once. Skim the scopes across the history and you get a tour of the whole application without opening it.

Let me give you one real example, because the abstract idea of a good message lands harder when you see one doing actual work. There's a fix in that Mac batch scope, and what it did was make a particular step safe to run twice. Here's the situation. When the app launched, it had a step that would try to resume jobs that were left unfinished. Fine. But sometimes a job was already running when that resume step fired, and the resume step would try to start it again, and now you've got two things fighting over the same work. A race, where two parts of the program reach the same spot at the same time and trip over each other.

The fix made that resume step idempotent. That's a useful word. Idempotent means you can do the thing once or do it five times and the result is the same. Like a lift button. Pressing it again when it's already lit doesn't summon two lifts. The resume step learned to check whether a job was already running, and if it was, to quietly do nothing, instead of barging in. And crucially, the body of that commit explains the race in plain English, the way I just did. So anyone reading the history later, including the person who wrote it, doesn't have to reverse-engineer the danger. The message hands it to them. That is the difference between "fixed stuff" and a letter to the future. One leaves you to guess. The other tells you what was on fire and how it got put out.

There's one more kind of line you'll find threaded through Echo's history, and it's worth naming because it's a small piece of honesty. Many of those commits carry a line at the bottom that credits an AI assistant as a co-author. It's called the Co-Authored-By line, and it does exactly what it sounds like, it attributes the work to more than one author. This app was built by a human working alongside an AI, and rather than quietly pretend otherwise, the history just says so, commit by commit. And that's another kind of context, isn't it. It's not about what changed, it's about how the work was done, and who, or what, did it. History is supposed to be true, and that line keeps it true.

Now let me circle back to one piece I set aside earlier, because it's where good commits are actually made, and it's the part beginners most often miss. Between editing your files and saving a commit, there's a staging area. You met the command for it, git add, the step where you choose what goes into the next commit. And the mental shift I want to leave you with is this. Staging isn't just saving. It's curating.

Here's what I mean. You don't have to commit everything you changed all at once. You can stage whole files, sure. But you can also stage individual sections within a single file, picking out just the lines that belong to one logical change and leaving the rest for the next commit. Which means that even if you wandered, even if you fixed the bug and tidied a function and adjusted some text all in the same sitting, you can still assemble clean, separate, single-purpose commits out of that messy session afterward. The staging area is your editing desk. It's where you take the raw sprawl of an afternoon's work and arrange it into bricks that each tell one clear story.

So the rhythm to aim for is: commit after each coherent piece of work, not at the end of the day in one giant heap. A commit is a publication, not an autosave. Autosave is the computer protecting you from losing work, and it happens silently, constantly, and it means nothing. A commit is you deciding that a piece of work is finished enough to put your name on and write a note about. Those are different acts. One is the machine being careful. The other is you being deliberate. And your history is the record of the deliberate ones.

If you want one test to carry with you, here it is, and it's the same question we started with. Look at the message you're about to write and ask: if I saw this in six months, would I know what it does and why? If yes, commit it. If no, you haven't finished the message yet. That's the whole bar. Not eloquence, not length. Just, would future me understand this. "Fixed stuff" fails that test in about half a second. "Fix crash when resuming playback after a phone call," with a body that explains the interrupted audio session, sails through.

Because that's what your history actually is. It's the autobiography of your project, written one commit at a time, by you, on purpose. Not a log that merely happens to you while you work, but a story you are choosing to tell, entry by entry, about how this thing came to exist and why it is the way it is. And future you is going to read that autobiography, probably on some stressed-out Tuesday night, hunting for the moment something went sideways. You get to decide right now whether that future reading is a relief or an aggravation. You decide it every time you reach for that message box.

So far, every commit we've talked about has landed in a single line of history, one after another, like beads on one string. But real work isn't always a straight line. Sometimes you want to try something risky without disturbing the version that already works, to start a new line of history off to the side, fiddle freely, and only fold it back in if it pans out. That sideways line has a name, and it's where we go next. It's called a branch.
Chapter 4 - Branching Out

Picture the feature that finally breaks your nerve. In Echo, it might be something like changing how a chapter remembers where you stopped listening. Sounds small. It isn't. That one idea reaches into the iPhone app, the Mac app, and the watch app. It touches the home-screen Widget, because the Widget shows your progress too. It reaches down into the Shared module that all of those build from, and maybe even into a database migration, one of those numbered schema changes that live in a folder called Shared slash Database slash Migrations. By the time you've sketched the whole thing out, you're looking at fifteen files across three platforms. This is not an afternoon. This is two weeks of evenings, squeezed in after a full day of carrying mail.

Here's the cruel part. The whole time you're building it, people are using Echo. They opened the app this morning to study on the bus. They expect it to work this afternoon. They are not going to wait three weeks while you finish your grand rework, and they will absolutely not forgive you if, halfway through, the app starts crashing because you tore out the old progress code before the new code was ready. You need to change everything, and you need to change nothing, both at the same time.

That contradiction is the problem branches were invented to solve. And honestly, most people don't go looking for branches out of foresight. They find them because they got burned. They edited the live thing directly, broke it, and spent a miserable night trying to remember what it used to look like. Maybe they'd been chipping away at three different ideas all jumbled together in the same files, and when one of them went wrong there was no clean way to peel it back out from the other two. Branches are the lesson you learn right after the night you wish you'd known about them. Let's make sure you learn it the easy way instead.

So what actually is a branch? This is where Git pleasantly refuses to be as scary as it sounds. When you hear "branch," your brain probably pictures a copy. A whole second version of the project, duplicated off to the side, so you can mess with one while the other stays safe. That mental picture is wrong, and the truth is much nicer.

A branch is a pointer. A tiny label. Think of all your commits as a long row of beads on a string, each bead a saved snapshot of the project, each one pointing back at the one before it. A branch is just a little tag clipped onto one of those beads, saying "this one." That tag is about forty-one bytes. Forty-one. That's smaller than this sentence. It is not a copy of your project, it's a sticky note that names a spot in your history. Hold that distinction, because it flips your whole intuition. A copy is something you'd hesitate to make, because copies are heavy and they pile up and they drift apart from each other until you can't tell which one is real. A sticky note is something you'd scribble without a second thought. Branches are sticky notes. That's why developers make them so casually, and why you should too.

Because it's just a sticky note, creating one is instant and costs basically nothing. You run the git branch command, give your branch a name, and it's done before your finger leaves the key. No copying. No waiting. No disk filling up. And here's the part that makes it actually useful: when you're standing on that branch and you make a new commit, the label moves forward to the new bead, all on its own. The branch you started from doesn't budge. It stays clipped exactly where it was, pointing at the version that still works, the one your users are happily running, while your label walks off into new territory carrying your half-finished progress feature.

So you can have one branch sitting on the safe, shipping version of Echo, and another branch walking forward through your risky rework, and they coexist with no conflict and no slowdown. You could have a dozen of these labels. Two dozen. Git won't even breathe hard, because they're all just little tags on the same string of beads. Once that clicks, branches stop feeling like a heavyweight operation you save for special occasions, and start feeling like what they are: cheap, disposable, everywhere.
Now, why would you actually reach for one? Three reasons keep coming up, and they map neatly onto the kinds of trouble a real project hands you.

The first is isolation. You want to do something risky, and you want a wall around it. Say you decide the way Echo loads transcripts is a mess and you're going to rewrite it from the ground up. That's a refactor, which is a fancy word for rebuilding the inside of something without changing what it does on the outside. It's the kind of work where things are broken for hours, sometimes days, before they're better, where the app won't even start for a stretch in the middle because you've pulled out the old engine and haven't bolted in the new one yet. You do not want that mess anywhere near the version people are using. So you branch. Your refactor lives on its own label, walled off, free to be broken as long as it needs to be, while the working version sits untouched on its own branch, calm as ever, still installable, still shippable, still exactly what your users have. Isolation means your experiment can fail loudly without taking anyone down with it.

The second reason is experimentation. Sometimes you don't even know if an idea will work. You've got a hunch. Maybe Echo would feel better if the watch app preloaded the next chapter before you finished the current one. Maybe it'd be a battery disaster. You don't know until you try. So you make a throwaway branch, a little sandbox, and you just try it. And here's the quietly liberating part: if it turns out to be a bad idea, you delete the branch, and nothing is lost. The version that works was never touched. You paid forty-one bytes and an evening of your time, and what you bought was knowledge, the certainty that this idea doesn't pan out. That's not a failure. That's a cheap experiment that did its job. A branch makes "let me just see" almost free, and that freedom is the safety net letting you be braver than you'd dare on the live version.

The third reason is parallel work, and this one shows up the instant your nice tidy plan gets interrupted by reality. You're three days into your big progress-tracking feature. Files are half-edited, your head is full of it. And someone reports that the Mac app crashes the moment you open Preferences. That can't wait two weeks for your feature. It needs fixing now. Without branches you'd be stuck, because your working files are a construction site, and you can't ship a fix out of a construction site. You'd be forced to either finish the feature first, while users keep hitting the crash, or throw away the evening's work to get back to clean ground. Neither is acceptable.

But with branches, there's a third door. You just step over to a fresh branch off the stable version, fix the crash there in its own clean space, ship that fix, and then come back to your feature exactly as you left it. Two streams of work, running side by side, neither stepping on the other. Now, Git does give you a way to temporarily shove your half-done work onto a holding shelf so you can switch contexts, and that shelf has its place. But for a fix that might itself take an hour or stretch across a couple of sessions, the holding shelf gets awkward fast. You forget what's on it. You stack two things on it and lose track of which is which. A separate branch is cleaner: the work has a name, it has a home, and it isn't balanced precariously on a shelf waiting to be knocked off.

Which brings us to a tool that takes parallel work and makes it genuinely comfortable: worktrees. Here's the friction that branches alone don't solve. Switching branches swaps out the files in your one project folder. Your editor's open tabs go stale. The mental map you'd built of where everything is gets scrambled. You've felt this if you've ever been yanked out of deep focus to handle something urgent, and then spent twenty minutes just rebuilding where you were. That rebuild is the real cost of switching, and it's paid in your attention, not your disk.

A worktree sidesteps it. A worktree lets you have several branches checked out at the same time, each in its own separate folder on disk, while they all quietly share one underlying Git database, one string of beads. So your big feature stays fully spread out across your editor in one folder, windows open, exactly as you left it, while the urgent Mac crash gets its own folder right next door with its own branch. You don't tear down your workspace to deal with the fire. You just open a second window. Your half-built feature doesn't even know the interruption happened.

Echo leans on this heavily, and keeps these extra worktrees tucked under a folder called dot-claude slash worktrees. That naming is a clue to what they're for: a lot of Echo's work is done with an AI assistant, and an assistant working on its own branch in its own worktree can churn away without ever disturbing the feature you've got open. Picture it concretely. In one window, your feature, files spread out, your train of thought intact. In another window, off in its own folder, the assistant is methodically working through a fix on its own branch, building, testing, editing, completely sealed off from your side. Neither of you is reaching into the other's files. Neither of you has to wait. And because both windows draw from the same single Git database underneath, when the assistant's work is done and proven, it slots back into the shared history cleanly, no awkward reconciling of two separate copies that drifted apart.

Same trick for an urgent fix, or for the AI assistant and an urgent fix both, all at once, each in its own clean room. The tradeoff worth naming, just once, is honest: every worktree is another real folder on disk, another copy of the working files to keep track of, so they cost a little space and a little tidiness. You can also confuse yourself if you forget which window is which and start editing in the wrong one. But what they buy back is the thing you can't easily get more of, your own focus, and that's a trade most developers happily make.

Now, whatever you're branching for, you have to name the thing, and names matter more than beginners expect. Git itself does not care what you call a branch. You could name them all after your cats. Git would dutifully clip the labels and never complain. But you are not naming branches for Git. You are naming them for the human who has to look at a list of branches later and instantly understand what each one is, and that human is almost always you, a few weeks from now, with no memory of what you were thinking tonight. Your future self is a collaborator, and you owe them a clear note.

So Echo follows simple naming conventions, little prefixes that announce a branch's intention before you read another word. A branch that adds something new gets a feature prefix. A branch that repairs something broken gets a fix prefix. And a branch where the AI assistant is doing the driving gets a claude prefix.
